label: he | die | time
sentence:
He of the Little Shell

Once upon a time, all the people of a certain country had died, excepting two helpless children, a baby boy and a little girl.

label: child | be | ∅
sentence: When their parents died, these children were asleep.

label: girl | be | first
sentence: The little girl, who was the elder, was the first to awake.

label: she | look | she
sentence: She looked around her, but seeing nobody beside her little brother, who lay smiling in his dreams, she quietly resumed her bed.

label: brother | move | end
sentence: At the end of ten days her brother moved, without opening his eyes.

label: he | change | position
sentence: At the end of ten days more he changed his position, lying on the other side, and in this way he kept on sleeping for a long time; and pleasant, too, must have been his dreams, for his little sister never looked at him that he was not quite a little heaven of smiles and flashing lights, which beamed about his head and filled the lodge with a strange splendor.

label: girl | grow | ∅
sentence: The girl soon grew to be a woman, but the boy increased in stature very slowly.

label: it | be | time
sentence: It was a long time before he could even creep, and he was well advancedin years before he could stand alone.

label: sister | make | ∅
sentence:
When he was able to walk, his sister made him a little bow and arrows, and hung around his neck a small shell, saying:

"You shall be called Dais Imid, or He of the Little Shell.

label: he | go | bow
sentence: Every day he would go out with his little bow, shooting at the small birds.

label: bird | be | tit
sentence: The first bird he killed was a tom-tit.

label: sister | be | ∅
sentence: His sister was highly pleased when he took it to her.

label: she | prepare | ∅
sentence: She carefully prepared and stuffed it, and put it away for him.

label: he | kill | squirrel
sentence: The next day he killed a red squirrel.

label: sister | preserve | this
sentence: His sister preserved this, too.

label: he | kill | partridge
sentence: The third day he killed a partridge, and this they had for their evening meal.

label: he | acquire | courage
sentence: After this he acquired more courage, and would venture some distance from home.

label: skill | increase | ∅
sentence: His skill and success as a hunter daily increased, and he killed the deer, bear, moose, and other large animals inhabiting the forest.

label: he | become | hunter
sentence: At last, although so very small of stature, he became a great hunter, and all that he shot he brought home and shared with his sister; and whenever he entered the lodge, a light beamed about his head and filled the place with a strange splendor.

label: he | arrive | year
sentence: He had now arrived at the years of manhood, but he still remained a perfect infant in size.

label: he | come | lake
sentence: One day, walking about in quest of game, he came to a small lake.

label: it | be | season
sentence: It was in the winter season; and upon the ice of the lake he saw a man of giant height, employed killing beavers.

label: he | feel | ∅
sentence: Comparing himself with this great man, he felt that he was no bigger than an insect.

label: he | seat | himself
sentence: He seated himself on the shore and watched his movements.

label: he | put | they
sentence: When the large man had killed many beavers, he put them on a hand-sled which he had, and pursued his way home.

label: hunter | follow | ∅
sentence: When he saw him retire, the dwarf hunter followed, and, wielding his magic shell, he cut off the tail of one of the beavers, and ran home with the prize.

label: giant | be | ∅
sentence: The giant, on reaching his lodge with his sled-load of beavers, was surprised to find one of them shorn of its tail.

label: hero | go | lake
sentence: The next day the little hero of the shell went to the same lake.

label: giant | load | sled
sentence: The giant, who had been busy there for some time, had already loaded his sled and commenced his return; but running nimbly forward and overtaking him, he succeeded in securing another of the beaver-tails.

label: giant | say | ∅
sentence: "I wonder," said the giant, on reaching his lodge and overlooking his beavers, "what dog it is that has thus cheated me.

label: i | make | point
sentence: Could I meet him, I would make his flesh quiver at the point of my javelin.

label: giant | forget | ∅
sentence:
"

The giant forgot that he had taken these very beavers out of a beaver-dam which belonged to the little shell-man and his sister, without permission.

label: he | pursue | hunting
sentence: The next day he pursued his hunting at the beaver-dam near the lake, and he was again followed by the little man with the shell.

label: giant | be | ∅
sentence: This time the giant was so nimble in his movements that he had nearly reached home before the Shell, make the best speed he could, could overtake him; but he was just in time to clip another beaver's tail before the sled slipped into the lodge.

label: giant | be | giant
sentence: The giant would have been a patient giant, indeed, if his anger had not been violent at these constant tricks played upon him.

label: ∅ | be | ∅
sentence: What vexed him most, was, that he could not get a sight of his enemy.

label: giant | look | ∅
sentence: The giant, giving vent to his feelings with many loud rumbling words, looked sharply around to see whether he could discover any tracks.

label: he | find | none
sentence: He could find none.

label: unknown | step | ∅
sentence: The unknown had stepped too lightly to leave the slightest mark behind.

label: giant | go | ∅
sentence: The next day the giant resolved to disappoint his mysterious follower by going to the beaver-dam very early; and accordingly, when the little shell man came to the place he found the fresh traces of his work, but the giant had already gone away.

label: he | follow | track
sentence: He followed hard upon his tracks, but he failed to overtake him.

label: stranger | be | front
sentence: When he of the little shell came in sight of the lodge, the stranger was in front of it, employed in skinning his beavers.

label: i | let | ∅
sentence:
As Dais-Imid stood looking at him—for he had been all this time invisible—he thought:

"I will let him have a view of me.

label: man | look | ∅
sentence:
"

Presently the man, who proved to be no less a personage than the celebrated giant, Manabozho, looked up and saw him.

label: manabozho | say | ∅
sentence: After regarding him with attention, "Who are you, little man?" said Manabozho.

label: i | have | mind
sentence: "I have a mind to kill you.

label: hero | reply | ∅
sentence:
"

The little hero of the shell replied:

"If you were to try to kill me you could not do it.

label: manabozho | grab | speech
sentence:
"

With this speech of the little man, Manabozho grabbed at him; but when he thought to have had him in his hand, he was gone.

label: manabozho | cry | ∅
sentence: "Where are you now, little man?" cried Manabozho.

label: dwarf | answer | girdle
sentence: "Here, under your girdle," answered the shell-dwarf; at which giant Manabozho, thinking to crush him, slapped down his great hand with all his might; but on unloosing his girdle he was disappointed at finding no dwarf there.

label: he | cry | rage
sentence: "Where are you now, little man?" he cried again, in a greater rage than ever.

label: dwarf | reply | ∅
sentence: the dwarf replied; whereupon the giant Manabozho seized himself by the finger and thumb at the place, and gave it a violent tweak; but as he immediately heard the voice of the dwarf at a distance upon the ground, he was satisfied that he had only pulled his own nose to no purpose.

label: manabozho | say | ∅
sentence: "Good-by, Manabozho," said the voice of the invisible dwarf.

label: ∅ | count | tail
sentence: "Count your beaver-tails, and you will find that I have taken another for my sister;" for he of the little shell never, in his wanderings or pastimes, forgot his sister and her wishes.

label: he | make | ∅
sentence: And as he went away he made himself visible once more, and a light beamed about his head and lit the air around him with a strange splendor; a circumstance which Manabozho, who was at times quite thick-headed and dull of apprehension, could no way understand.

label: he | tell | sister
sentence: When Dais-Imid returned home, he told his sister that the time drew nigh when they must separate.

label: it | be | fate
sentence: "I must go away," said Dais-Imid, "it is my fate.

label: ∅ | go | ∅
sentence: You, too," he added, "must go away soon.

label: ∅ | tell | i
sentence: Tell me where you would wish to dwell.

label: she | say | ∅
sentence:
"

She said, "I would like to go to the place of the breaking of daylight.

label: i | love | east
sentence: I have always loved the East.

label: glimpse | be | quarter
sentence: The earliest glimpses of light are from that quarter, and it is to my mind the most beautiful part of the heavens.

label: you | think | direction
sentence: After I get there, my brother, whenever you see the clouds, in that direction, of various colors, you may think that your sister is painting her face."

label: i | say | ∅
sentence: "And I," said he, "I, my sister, shall live on the mountains and rocks.

label: air | be | ∅
sentence: There I can see you at the earliest hour; there are the streams of water clear; the air is pure, and the golden lights will shine ever around my head, and I shall ever be called 'Puck-Ininee, or the Little Wild Man of the Mountains.'

label: i | go | ∅
sentence: But," he resumed, "before we part forever, I must go and try to find what manitoes rule the earth, and see which of them will be friendly to us.

label: he | leave | sister
sentence:
"

He left his sister and traveled over the surface of the globe, and then went far down into the earth.

label: he | treat | ∅
sentence: He had been treated well wherever he went.

label: he | come | last
sentence: At last he came to a giant manito, who had a large kettle which was forever boiling.

label: he | throw | he
sentence: The giant, who was a first cousin to Manabozho, and had already heard of the tricks which Dais-Imid had played upon his kinsman, regarded him with a stern look, and, catching him up in his hand, he threw him unceremoniously into the kettle.

label: it | be | intention
sentence: It was evidently the giant's intention to drown Dais-Imid; in which he was mistaken, for by means of his magic shell, little Dais, in less than a second's time, bailed the water to the bottom, leaped from the kettle, and ran away unharmed.

label: he | return | sister
sentence: He returned to his sister and related his rovings and adventures.

label: sister | be | manito
sentence:
He finished his story by addressing her thus:

"My sister there is a manito at each of the four corners of the earth.

label: ∅ | be | one
sentence: There is also one above them, far in the sky, a Great Being who assigns to you, and-to me, and to all of us, where we must go.

label: ∅ | be | another
sentence: And last," he continued, "there is another and wicked one who lives deep down in the earth.

label: it | be | lot
sentence: It will be our lot to escape out of his reach.

label: we | separate | ∅
sentence: We must now separate.

label: you | go | ∅
sentence: When the winds blow from the four corners of the earth, you must then go.

label: they | carry | you
sentence: They will carry you to the place you wish.

label: i | go | rock
sentence:
I go to the rocks and mountains, where my kindred will ever delight to dwell."

Dais-Imid then took his ball-stick and commenced running up a high mountain, and a bright light shone about his head all the way, and he kept singing as he went:
Blow, winds, blow!

label: sister | linger | dwelling
sentence:
my sister lingers
For her dwelling in the sky,
Where the morn, with rosy fingers,

label: take | reflect | ∅
sentence:
There my earliest views directed,
Shall from her their color take,
And her smiles, through clouds reflected,
Guide me on by wood or lake.

label: sport | raise | hallo
sentence:
While I range the highest mountains,
Sport in valleys green and low,
Or, beside our Indian fountains,
Raise my tiny hip-hallo.

label: wind | blow | ∅
sentence: Presently the winds blew, and, as Dais-Imid had predicted, his sister was borne by them to the eastern sky, where she has ever since lived, and her name is now the Morning Star.
